questions.text: I am comfortable vetoing a group decision when I believe it is wrong.
I feel things so deeply that it can be physically exhausting.
I find that I can help people feel heard and valued within a group.
I trust the group's collective review process without needing personal approval authority.
I tend to evaluate opportunities primarily by how much they help me reach my goals.
I routinely say no to requests that don't align with my personal objectives.
I can tell when someone volunteers information to create an impression of transparency while still hiding the important parts.
I am rarely at a neutral emotional baseline — something is always registering strongly.
I focus on creating the conditions for a great present and let the future emerge from that.
I notice that I can be fully engaged in a situation without my emotional volume increasing.
I experience the world as though my emotional volume knob is permanently set several notches above average.
I hold the view that reliability is one of the most important qualities a person can have.
I believe that the capacity to evaluate suffering without emotional contamination is what makes fair and scalable responses to human need possible.
I tend to donate my old belongings rather than sell them, even when I could use the money.
I hold the view that someone must ultimately be accountable, and I accept that role.
I experience the world with a kind of emotional clarity that comes from not being flooded.
I hold the view that the moral weight of suffering should not be adjusted downward because of how common or expected the suffering is.
I sense when a question is being asked to confirm something the asker already knows, as a test.
I find it natural to sit back and let the group work through its process.
My startle response is mild — unexpected events register but don't jolt me.
I believe that when I process distressing situations through a lens of rational analysis, I am better able to protect the interests of all parties, not just the most vocal.
I believe that the moral significance of another person's pain should override considerations of personal cost.
I find that I often have to ask people directly how they are feeling because I cannot tell.
I think about what skills I'll need in the future and start developing them now.
I focus on the explicit agenda of a meeting rather than the interpersonal dynamics.
I believe that a well-furnished personal space is a worthwhile investment.
I prefer to formulate my response to a difficult human situation before engaging with its emotional content.
I mix up different types of tasks throughout the day to keep things fresh.
I do not usually detect when someone is using a personal anecdote as an indirect warning.
I find that I am constantly scanning faces for emotional information.
I feel most aligned with my values when I am serving a purpose bigger than myself.
I believe that my willingness to decide quickly is valuable to those around me.
I feel that the effort of maintaining order is always worth the payoff in clarity.
I find that the moral demand of suffering grows stronger, not weaker, the more carefully I consider it.
I believe that self-sacrifice is not a burden but a calling.
I assess progress by how things feel rather than by numerical metrics.
